Offense Description: Restoration of Firearm Rights – State and federal processes for restoring the right to possess firearms after a felony conviction; state restoration does not automatically restore federal rights.
Firearm Rights Restorable: Conditional — state restoration may be possible for state-only offenses; federal prohibition under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g) requires Presidential pardon or ATF petition (program unfunded)
License Impact: Hunting and concealed carry licenses cannot be restored while federal prohibition remains active
BTB Status: Excluded — federal prohibition bars firearms possession regardless of state action
Key Collateral Consequences Raw: Arkansas state law: no specific statutory restoration mechanism for state felony firearms bar; federal law (18 U.S.C. § 925(c)) allows petitions to ATF but program is unfunded; Presidential pardon is the only reliable path to federal firearm rights restoration; expungement under state law does NOT restore federal firearm rights; Class D felony (non-violent, state only) may have different analysis under state law
Collateral Firearms: Federal prohibition under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g) remains even after state restoration; ATF § 925(c) program is unfunded; possession of firearm while prohibited is a federal felony
Collateral Employment: Firearms-related employment (law enforcement, security) permanently barred while federal prohibition remains
Source Citation: Ark. Code Ann. § 5-73-103 / 18 U.S.C. § 922(g) | Firearms Rights Restoration

Offense Description: Employer Access to Sealed Records – Statutory framework governing which employers and agencies retain lawful access to sealed criminal records notwithstanding sealing order.
License Impact: Professional licensing boards retain access; sealed record may be considered in licensing determinations
BTB Status: Public employers and licensed-position employers retain access; private employers generally do not
Key Collateral Consequences Raw: Agencies retaining access to sealed records: (1) Law enforcement agencies; (2) Courts; (3) Department of Human Services; (4) Arkansas Department of Education; (5) Professional licensing boards; (6) Federally regulated employers (banking, healthcare, defense contractors); (7) Employers with court-authorized access. Private employers generally do not retain access without court order. Sealed records cannot be used as basis for denial of employment in private sector without court authorization.
Collateral Employment: Private employers: access denied without court order; licensed employers and public agencies: retain access
Source Citation: Ark. Code Ann. § 16-90-1401(e) | Comprehensive Criminal Record Sealing Act of 2013

Offense Description: Certificate of Rehabilitation – Court-issued document demonstrating rehabilitation; may mitigate collateral consequences for employment and licensing even where full expungement is unavailable.
License Impact: Rehabilitation evidence considered in discretionary license reviews; not binding but persuasive for boards with discretionary authority
BTB Status: No formal BTB protection but may reduce employment screening barriers
Key Collateral Consequences Raw: Arkansas does not have a formal Certificate of Rehabilitation statute equivalent to California or New York; however, courts may issue letters of rehabilitation or recommendation in support of pardon or licensing applications; Board of Corrections may issue compliance certificates; these are persuasive but not legally binding on licensing boards; some professional boards give weight to demonstrated rehabilitation in discretionary licensing reviews
Collateral Employment: May support employment applications where discretionary hiring authority exists
Source Citation: Ark. Code Ann. § 16-93-1001 | Post-Conviction Relief / Rehabilitation Evidence
